data.description:

October Durable Goods Orders Fall as Aircraft Demand Tumbles
Canada GDP Edges Up After Sharp Contraction in October -- 2nd Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart


OTTAWA--Canada's economy is on track to shrink slightly in the final quarter of 2025, with the steepest contraction in almost three years notched in October and early signs pointing to only a modest recovery last month.

Gross domestic product decreased 0.3% in October from the month before, Statistics Canada said Tuesday. That reverses the 0.2% advance in September and marks the biggest step down for the economy since the end of 2022.

The national data agency's advance estimate for November indicates growth of 0.1%.

The final major economic indicator of the year sounded a sour note after recent jobs data showed hiring has picked up strongly in recent months, helping cool a still-elevated unemployment rate, and inflation figures show price pressures are largely contained.

The data on GDP, a broad measure of the goods and services produced across the economy, suggests the economy carried little to no momentum after the stronger-than-expected rebound in activity in the third quarter. If December were to show no growth, the current pace would have the economy headed for a contraction of about 0.5% on an annualized basis for the fourth quarter.

"The Canadian economy has some work cut out to avoid another negative print for the final quarter of the year. That will close out a very choppy year for Canadian growth in still-choppy fashion," Robert Kavcic, senior economist at Bank of Montreal Capital Markets, said.

The Bank of Canada, which has signaled it is done lowering interest rates after a string of aggressive moves, has forecast the economy will grow a modest 0.5% on an annual basis in the final quarter of 2025 and then expand an average 1.4% over 2026 and 2027 as weakness in the labor market weighs on household spending. The economy's potential has been badly dented by the trade conflict with the U.S. and the heightened uncertainty that has trailed President Trump's changeable tariffs.

Statistics Canada's flash estimate for November indicated recoveries in educational services, construction and transportation. That looks to have been partially offset by weakness in mining and oil-and-gas extraction and a further decline in manufacturing, which has faced a particularly rough year thanks to tariffs.

The data agency on Tuesday said an advance tally of wholesale receipts showed sales ticked up 0.1% on the month before in November, while its prior estimate for retail trade pointed to a 1.2% rise in sales, which would be the strongest advance in five months.

October's industry accounts showed a broad decline in goods-producing industries, led by a drop in manufacturing that largely offset the sector's expansion the month before.

There also was weakness during the month for the oil-and-gas industry following four straight months of expansion, and construction activity declined for the first time in six months.

Labor disputes also played a part in October's pullback. A province-wide strike in Alberta for much of October led to a contraction for the public sector, while a nationwide strike by Canada Post carriers disrupted deliveries as a walkout that began in late September shifted to rotating strikes Oct. 11.

On the positive side for the economy, the Toronto Blue Jays' World Series playoff run was likely behind strength in the arts, entertainment and recreation sector and in accommodation and food-services activity. And the finance-and-insurance industry saw a fifth consecutive monthly increase, with activity hitting a fresh record high.

The data shows economic activity remains volatile, and that the recovery from the hit taken from U.S. tariffs, which kicked in earlier in the year, remains uncertain.

Michael Davenport, senior Canada economist at Oxford Economics, said growth is likely to be restricted to the low-single-digits range on a quarterly basis in the near term, leaving the economy vulnerable to further trade disruptions and other shocks. "Although the economy avoided a technical recession in 2025, we expect weak underlying momentum to carry through the first half 2026 amid U.S. tariffs, elevated trade policy uncertainty and a shrinking population," he said.


Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

12-23-25 1100ET

data.title: Canada April-October budget deficit grows by almost 27%
Lawsuit challenges Trump administration's ending of protections for South Sudanese migrants
data.description:
BOSTON, Dec 23 (Reuters) - Immigrant rights advocates have filed a lawsuit challenging the Trump administration's decision last month to end the temporary protections from deportation granted to more than 200 South Sudanese nationals.

Four migrants from South Sudan, along with the non-profit African Communities Together, alleged in a lawsuit filed in Boston federal court on Monday that the U.S. Department of Homeland Security was unlawfully putting them at risk of losing their temporary protected status after January 5.

That status, known as TPS, is available to people whose home countries have experienced natural disasters, armed conflicts or other extraordinary events. It provides eligible migrants with work authorization and temporary protection from deportation.

The lawsuit argues the agency's action violated the statute governing the TPS program, ignored the dire humanitarian conditions that remain in South Sudan, and was motivated by discrimination against migrants who are not white in violation of the U.S. Constitution's Fifth Amendment.

"This pattern reveals the administration's true agenda: stripping protections from immigrant communities of color regardless of the dangers they face," Amaha Kassa, the executive director of African Communities Together, said in a statement.

The Department of Homeland Security did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

South Sudan has faced repeated bouts of violent conflict since 2011, with a civil war between 2013 and 2018 killing 400,000 people. The United States began designating South Sudan for TPS in 2011.

About 232 South Sudanese nationals have been beneficiaries of TPS and have found refuge in the United States, and another 73 have pending applications for that same protection, according to the lawsuit.

Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, on November 5, published a notice terminating TPS for South Sudan, saying the country no longer met the conditions for the designation.

She did so after her department moved to similarly end temporary protections extended to foreign nationals from countries including Syria, Venezuela, Haiti, Cuba and Nicaragua, prompting several court challenges.

(Reporting by Nate Raymond in BostonEditing by Rod Nickel)

By Nate Raymond

data.title: RBC Says Alberta's Bitumen Production Crosses 2.0 Million BBL/D in November

U.S. Mid-Atlantic Factory Activity Stays Slow Despite Brightening Outlook -- Richmond Fed
data.description:
By Ed Frankl


Manufacturing activity in the mid-Atlantic region contracted again, but at a less sharp clip this month, as conditions broadly improved and expectations for future business picked up pace.

The Fifth District Survey of Manufacturing Activity's composite manufacturing index rose eight points to minus 7 in December, the Federal Reserve Bank of Richmond said Tuesday. Economists polled by The Wall Street Journal had expected a weaker minus 10.

The reading below zero means more of the 149-165 firms responding to the survey reported decreasing activity than those reporting an increase. The last increase was in February.

All three of the component indexes--shipments, new orders and employment--increased from November but remained negative, the Richmond Fed said.

The index for local current business conditions climbed to minus 9 from minus 20 in November, though still registered a contraction in December. However, for local business sixth months from now, expectations gathered pace to a reading of 16 in December, from 1 last month, the survey said.

Meanwhile, average growth rates of prices decreased slightly in December, while firms expected increases in prices paid to moderate and growth in prices received to fall slightly over the next 12 months, according to the Richmond Fed.

Firms surveyed are located within the Fifth Federal Reserve District, which includes the District of Columbia, Maryland, North Carolina, South Carolina, Virginia, and most of West Virginia.


Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

12-23-25 1051ET

data.title: ServiceNow, Inc. agreed to acquire Armis Inc. for $7.8 billion.
data.description:
ServiceNow, Inc. (NYSE:NOW) agreed to acquire Armis Inc. for $7.8 billion on December 23, 2025. The consideration is subject to customary adjustments. ServiceNow expects to fund the transaction through a combination of cash on hand and debt.

The transaction is expected to close in the second half of 2026, subject to customary regulatory approvals and closing conditions.

Tidal Partners LLC,J.P. Morgan Securities LLC and Barclays Capital Inc. acted as financial advisor for ServiceNow, Inc.

data.title: Philippe Donnet Sells 200,000 Generali Shares
data.description:
(Alliance News) - Assicurazioni Generali Spa announced on Tuesday that Philippe Donnet, Group Chief Executive Officer, has sold 200,000 ordinary shares of the company.

The shares were sold at an average unit price of EUR35.632, for a total value of EUR7.1 million.

Assicurazioni Generali shares are down slightly at EUR35.99 per share.

By Antonio Di Giorgio, Alliance News reporter

Verisure Board Member Luis Gil Purchases Shares Worth 2.8 Million Euros (Update)
data.description:
(update: an additional purchase of 195,426 shares has been reported)

On December 19, Luis Gil, through the company Securholds Spain, acquired 200,766 shares in the security company Verisure, where he serves as a board member. The shares were bought at a price of 13.95 euros each, amounting to a transaction worth 2.8 million euros. The deal was conducted on Nasdaq Stockholm. This information is disclosed in the Financial Supervisory Authority's insider registry.

As of December 18, Luis Gil owned 14.42 million shares, representing 1.39 percent of the capital and votes in Verisure, according to the shareholder service Holdings.

data.title: Consumer Confidence Falls as Jobs, Economic Worries Persist
data.description:
By Ed Frankl


Consumer sentiment in the U.S. unexpectedly weakened for a fifth consecutive month in December, amid enduring concerns over jobs and business conditions, a monthly survey said.

Research group The Conference Board said Tuesday its consumer confidence index inched down to 89.1 from 92.9 in November. Economists polled by The Wall Street Journal had expected a higher reading of 91.0. November's data was revised up as responses collected after the end of the federal-government shutdown were more positive than those collected during the impasse, the survey said.

"Despite an upward revision in November related to the end of the shutdown, consumer confidence fell again in December and remained well below this year's January peak," said Dana M. Peterson, chief economist at The Conference Board.

Consumers' assessment of business and labor-market conditions plummeted, with views on current business conditions negative for the first time since September 2024, the survey said.

The expectations index, based on short-term outlook for income, business, and labor market conditions, held steady. However, it has now tracked below the threshold which the gauge signals recession ahead for 11 straight months.

The decline in confidence contrasts a little with the results of the University of Michigan's closely watched December consumer confidence survey published last week. Mood improved slightly in December, though remains at a weak level as consumers fret over inflation and a creaking labor market, it said.

The Conference Board's data also come after the U.S. economy was shown to have expanded at a faster pace than anticipated in the third quarter. Gross domestic product rose 4.3% on an annualized basis, up from 3.8% in the previous quarter.


Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

12-23-25 1043ET

Silver hits record above $70, gold eases after US data lifts dollar
data.description:
Dec 23 (Reuters) - Silver extended its record rally on Tuesday and hit the key $70 an ounce mark, while gold cut gains after setting an all-time high as stronger-than-expected U.S. economic data sparked a pullback.

Spot silver rose 0.7% at $69.48 per ounce after touching a record high of $70.66 earlier. Prices are up 123% year-to-date. 

"At the bottom of this reality of supply and demand in a market that has been in deficit for five years, alongside increasing industrial demand. The safe-haven aspect, expectations of a weaker dollar and lower yields is part of that equation," said Peter Grant, vice president and senior metals strategist at Zaner Metals.

"Silver's next target is $75 but year-end profit-taking could trigger a pullback." 

The U.S. dollar trimmed its losses after data showed the world's largest economy grew faster than expected in the third quarter. A firmer dollar makes dollar-priced metals less attractive to overseas buyers. [USD/][US/]  

Spot gold edged up 0.1% to $4,449.99 per ounce, as of 9:57 a.m. ET (1457 GMT), after hitting a record $4,497.55. Prices pared gains after the data. 

Bullion has surged about 70% this year, driven by geopolitical tensions, U.S. rate cuts, strong central bank buying, and robust investment demand.

U.S. gold futures for February delivery gained 0.3% to $4,480.20.     

"We continue to see the longer-term thematic of central bank foreign reserve diversification as a major tailwind for gold prices through the end of the decade," analysts at SP Angel said in a note. 

"We see gold rising towards $5,000/ounce next year," the note added.   

In the latest flare-up in geopolitical tensions, U.S. President Donald Trump last week ordered a "blockade" of all sanctioned oil tankers entering and leaving Venezuela, and said he was not ruling out the possibility of war with the country.

The Trump administration said it will impose tariffs on Chinese semiconductor imports starting in June 2027.

Spot platinum jumped 3.9% to $2,202.30, its highest in over 17 years.

Palladium rose 2.8% to a three-year high of $1,808.25, tracking strength in gold and silver.

(Reporting by Sarah Qureshi and Ashitha Shivaprasad in Bengaluru; Editing by Tasim Zahid)

By Sarah Qureshi

data.title: Presenter Anna Hedenmo Leaves EFN
data.description:
Presenter Anna Hedenmo is leaving Handelsbanken's financial channel EFN, reports Dagens Media. Last summer, Hedenmo departed from SVT after 30 years.

"My contract with EFN expires this spring, and both parties agreed to bring it to a close. The reason is that the new management wants an even clearer focus on the stock market and finance, and since I was recruited to do what I do best—interviewing politicians—it's better for me to move on," she wrote to the newspaper.

Verisure Board Member Luis Gil Increases His Shareholding
data.description:
On December 19, through his company Securholds Spain, Luis Gil purchased 5,340 shares in the security company Verisure, where he serves as a board member. The shares were acquired at a price of 13.92 euro per share, amounting to a total transaction value of 74,300 euro. The transaction was carried out on Nasdaq Stockholm. This information is disclosed in the Swedish Financial Supervisory Authority's (Finansinspektionen) insider register.

As of December 18, Luis Gil owned 14.42 million shares, corresponding to 1.39 percent of the capital and voting rights in Verisure, according to the shareholder service Holdings.
